text:
HEMATOLOGY/MEDICAL ONCOLOGY — OUTPATIENT FOLLOW-UP NOTE

ATTENDING PHYSICIAN: Medical Oncology
VISIT TYPE: Established Patient — Disease Reassessment and Ongoing Treatment Management

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Mr. Delgado is a 59-year-old Hispanic male with stage IV (cT3 N2b M1b) sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma (KRAS G12D mutant, MSS) with hepatic and pulmonary metastases, presenting today for routine follow-up and review of recent restaging CT imaging while on second-line FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Mr. Marcus Delgado is a 59-year-old former smoker and high school teacher with a well-established diagnosis of stage IV metastatic colorectal cancer, now approximately 14 months from initial diagnosis. He was originally diagnosed with a 4.5 cm sigmoid colon primary with synchronous bilobar liver metastases (four lesions, largest 3.8 cm) and was found to harbor a KRAS G12D driver mutation, precluding anti-EGFR therapy. His comprehensive NGS also identified co-mutations in APC, TP53, SMAD4, and PIK3CA, along with MYC amplification, and confirmed MSS/low-TMB disease.

His first-line treatment consisted of FOLFOX plus bevacizumab for 12 planned cycles. He achieved a partial response by imaging after 4 cycles (CEA trending from 487 down to 198 ng/mL; target lesion sum reduced by approximately 25%). Unfortunately, he developed progressive peripheral neuropathy attributed to oxaliplatin — progressing through grade 1 (cycle 3) to grade 2 (cycle 8) and ultimately grade 3 (cycle 10, with bilateral sensorimotor involvement to mid-calf and wrists), necessitating early discontinuation of oxaliplatin after a cumulative dose of approximately 760 mg/m2. He completed his induction course as modified FOLFOX (5-FU/LV/bevacizumab) and transitioned to maintenance 5-FU/leucovorin/bevacizumab. EMG/nerve conduction studies confirmed axonal, length-dependent oxaliplatin-induced neuropathy.

While on maintenance therapy, restaging imaging approximately 10 months from diagnosis identified disease progression in the form of two new pulmonary nodules (right lower lobe 1.1 cm, right upper lobe 0.7 cm), subsequently confirmed as metastatic colorectal adenocarcinoma by CT-guided core needle biopsy. Hepatic lesions showed minimal interval increase. CEA had rebounded to 142 ng/mL. This triggered transition to second-line therapy.

After multidisciplinary tumor board review, the decision was made to initiate FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab (per the RAISE trial regimen), given prior oxaliplatin exposure/toxicity and bevacizumab use in the first-line setting. His UGT1A1 genotype was *1/*28 (heterozygous), indicating an intermediate metabolizer status, and he was started at standard irinotecan dosing with close toxicity monitoring.

He is now approximately 4 months into second-line therapy (roughly 8 cycles of FOLFIRI/ramucirumab, though irinotecan was dose-reduced to 150 mg/m2 from cycle 4 onward due to grade 2 delayed diarrhea after cycle 3). He also experienced grade 3 hypertension attributed to ramucirumab at cycle 5, which required a one-cycle hold of ramucirumab and intensification of his antihypertensive regimen; ramucirumab was subsequently resumed at full dose after blood pressure was better controlled.

A restaging CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed approximately 1-2 weeks ago (after 4 cycles of the FOLFIRI/ramucirumab combination) and the results were reviewed with the patient today. The scan showed disease stabilization: the right lower lobe lung lesion was unchanged at 1.1 cm and the right upper lobe lesion unchanged at 0.7 cm; no new pulmonary nodules. Liver metastases showed a slight decrease in the segment VI lesion (2.3 cm from 2.5 cm) with the remaining three hepatic lesions stable. The sigmoid primary was essentially unchanged at 3.1 cm. The overall sum of target lesions was 5.7 cm compared with 6.1 cm at the time of second-line initiation — representing approximately 7% decrease, consistent with stable disease. CEA was 112 ng/mL, slightly improved from 142 ng/mL at the time of progression.

The patient reports his overall functional status has been relatively preserved. He is fatigued on a day-to-day basis but able to carry out most activities of daily living with some modifications. He has returned to part-time teaching duties, which he finds meaningful and mentally engaging. He continues to experience residual peripheral neuropathy in his bilateral feet, now grade 1 (intermittent tingling, not interfering with ambulation), significantly improved from the grade 3 neuropathy at its nadir. Gabapentin has been gradually tapered and the patient is currently on a reduced dose.

Diarrhea has been manageable with dose reduction and loperamide use as directed. He denies new abdominal pain, rectal bleeding, or significant change in bowel habits beyond his irinotecan-related loose stools. His appetite is reasonably maintained and he has not had significant unintentional weight loss since the last visit.

Today's visit includes a detailed goals-of-care discussion. The patient expressed that he was aware his cancer was incurable and that the goal of treatment was to control the disease and maintain quality of life as long as possible. He expressed a strong desire to continue active anticancer therapy if his body can tolerate it, and emphasized the importance of staying present and functional for his family — his wife and two adult children. He was receptive to palliative care involvement and agreed to proceed with an outpatient palliative care consultation today, as well as a formal palliative care referral for ongoing symptom management co-management going forward.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
- Constitutional: Moderate fatigue, managed. No fever, chills, or night sweats. Appetite fair, stable weight.
- Neurological: Residual bilateral lower extremity tingling, grade 1. No new neurological symptoms. No headache, dizziness, or visual changes.
- Gastrointestinal: Loose stools 2-3 per day (manageable with loperamide, improved from prior grade 2 diarrhea). Mild intermittent nausea on infusion days, controlled with ondansetron. No vomiting, hematemesis, melena, or hematochezia. No significant abdominal pain. No jaundice.
- Cardiovascular/Vascular: Hypertension under management (see medications). No chest pain, shortness of breath, or lower extremity edema. No thromboembolic events.
- Pulmonary: Mild exertional dyspnea on significant effort, not limiting. No hemoptysis, pleuritic chest pain, or new cough.
- Musculoskeletal: Mild lower extremity weakness attributed to deconditioning. No bone pain.
- Dermatologic: No rash or hand-foot syndrome.
- Genitourinary: No hematuria or urinary symptoms.
- Endocrine: Diabetes mellitus well-controlled (patient reports monitoring home glucose, levels 110-140 mg/dL fasting).
- Psychological/Social: Patient reports intermittent anxiety about prognosis, manages with family support. No active depression identified; mood largely preserved. Coping well overall.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
- Stage IV (cT3 N2b M1b) sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma with hepatic and pulmonary metastases — as detailed above
- Type 2 diabetes mellitus — well-controlled on metformin (diagnosed approximately 8 years ago)
- Hypertension — now requiring triple antihypertensive therapy (bevacizumab- and ramucirumab-related exacerbation)
- Oxaliplatin-induced peripheral neuropathy — grade 3 at nadir, now improving to grade 1

SURGICAL HISTORY:
- No prior abdominal surgeries
- Colonoscopy with biopsies (diagnostic, at time of presentation)
- CT-guided core needle biopsy of right lower lobe pulmonary nodule (at time of progression, confirmed metastatic CRC)

FAMILY HISTORY:
- Father: colorectal cancer (age of onset not specified); patient's germline NGS was unremarkable for Lynch syndrome or FAP-associated variants
- No other known first-degree family history of malignancy

SOCIAL HISTORY:
- Former smoker: approximately 20 pack-year history, quit 12 years prior to current diagnosis
- Occupation: high school teacher; currently working part-time
- Marital status: married; lives at home with wife
- Two adult children, both supportive and involved in care decisions
- Alcohol: occasional, minimal; no recreational drug use
- Functional status: ECOG Performance Status 1
- Insurance: adequate; no financial toxicity concerns raised at this visit

ALLERGIES:
- No known drug allergies

MEDICATIONS:
1. Metformin 1000 mg orally twice daily (type 2 diabetes)
2. Amlodipine 10 mg orally daily (hypertension — bevacizumab/ramucirumab-related; previously titrated)
3. Lisinopril 20 mg orally daily (hypertension — added and uptitrated during FOLFIRI/ramucirumab)
4. Hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg orally daily (hypertension — added cycle 5 of FOLFIRI/ramucirumab for uncontrolled BP)
5. Gabapentin 300 mg orally twice daily (being tapered from 300 mg TID; initiated for oxaliplatin neuropathy)
6. Irinotecan 150 mg/m2 IV every 2 weeks (dose-reduced from 180 mg/m2 per protocol — part of FOLFIRI backbone)
7. Leucovorin 400 mg/m2 IV every 2 weeks (part of FOLFIRI backbone)
8. 5-Fluorouracil 400 mg/m2 IV bolus + 2400 mg/m2 continuous infusion over 46 hours (FOLFIRI backbone)
9. Ramucirumab 8 mg/kg IV every 2 weeks (second-line antiangiogenic agent; held cycle 5 for grade 3 HTN, since resumed)
10. Ondansetron 8 mg orally every 8 hours PRN nausea (infusion-related)
11. Loperamide 4 mg at first loose stool, then 2 mg every 2 hours PRN diarrhea (irinotecan-related diarrhea protocol)
12. Atropine 0.25 mg subcutaneous PRN (acute cholinergic reaction to irinotecan — administered prophylactically at infusion center)

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:

Vital Signs:
- Blood pressure: 138/86 mmHg (right arm, seated — acceptable control on current antihypertensive regimen)
- Heart rate: 74 bpm, regular
- Respiratory rate: 16 breaths/min
- Temperature: 98.4°F (36.9°C)
- Weight: 174 lbs (79.1 kg; stable from prior visit)
- BMI: 26.4 kg/m2
- ECOG Performance Status: 1

General: Well-appearing, well-nourished Hispanic male in no acute distress. Alert and oriented times three. Pleasant and engaged throughout the encounter.

HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. No scleral icterus. Oral mucosa moist, no mucositis or ulcerations.

Neck: No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy palpable.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema.

Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rhonchi, or crackles. No dullness to percussion suggesting pleural effusion.

Abdomen: Soft, mildly tender to deep palpation in the left lower quadrant (consistent with sigmoid primary location), non-distended. No guarding, no rigidity. Liver edge palpable approximately 1-2 cm below the right costal margin; no hepatic tenderness elicited. No splenomegaly. No ascites appreciated on exam (no shifting dullness or fluid wave).

Extremities: No clubbing or cyanosis. Trace bilateral ankle edema (new, possibly medication-related or nutritional). Capillary refill less than 2 seconds.

Neurological: Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Motor strength 5/5 in bilateral upper and lower extremities. Decreased light touch and vibratory sensation in bilateral feet to the ankle level (consistent with residual length-dependent neuropathy, significantly improved from prior exam). Gait antalgic but without significant ataxia. Romberg negative.

Lymph Nodes: No axillary, inguinal, or cervical lymphadenopathy appreciated.

Skin: No rash, jaundice, or signs of hand-foot syndrome.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS (recent, from today's draw or from most recent available):

Complete Blood Count:
- WBC: 5.8 x10^9/L (within normal limits)
- Hemoglobin: 10.9 g/dL (mild anemia, stable — likely anemia of chronic disease/malignancy, no new decline)
- Hematocrit: 33.2%
- Platelets: 218 x10^9/L
- ANC: 2.4 x10^9/L (adequate)

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium: 138 mEq/L
- Potassium: 3.9 mEq/L
- Creatinine: 0.92 mg/dL (eGFR >60, adequate renal function)
- BUN: 16 mg/dL
- Total bilirubin: 0.8 mg/dL
- ALT: 38 U/L (mildly elevated, consistent with hepatic tumor burden)
- AST: 44 U/L (mildly elevated, hepatic involvement)
- Alkaline phosphatase: 112 U/L (mildly elevated, stable)
- Albumin: 3.6 g/dL (low-normal, reflects nutritional status and chronic disease)

Tumor Markers:
- CEA: 112 ng/mL (decreased from 142 ng/mL at prior check; trending in the right direction)
- CA 19-9: 94 U/mL (decreased from 108 U/mL; also mildly improving)

HbA1c: 6.9% (diabetes well-controlled)

Coagulation: PT/INR 1.1 (normal)

Urinalysis: No proteinuria (important monitoring point given antiangiogenic therapy)

---

IMAGING RESULTS:
Most recent CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast, reviewed today. As noted above:
- Pulmonary nodules: right lower lobe 1.1 cm and right upper lobe 0.7 cm — both stable compared to the scan obtained at the time second-line therapy was initiated. No new pulmonary nodules identified.
- Hepatic metastases: segment VI lesion marginally decreased to 2.3 cm (from 2.5 cm); segments IVa (1.1 cm), II (0.7 cm), and VII (0.4 cm) stable. No new hepatic lesions.
- Primary sigmoid tumor: 3.1 cm, essentially unchanged.
- No peritoneal nodularity, no ascites, no suspicious lymphadenopathy beyond what was previously noted.
- No new osseous lesions.
- Overall: stable disease. The hepatic and pulmonary disease is not growing, and the CEA/CA 19-9 are tracking favorably compared to the nadir of progression at the 10-month mark.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

Mr. Delgado is a 59-year-old man with stage IV, KRAS G12D mutant, MSS sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma with hepatic and pulmonary metastases, now approximately 14 months from initial diagnosis. He is currently on second-line FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab and demonstrates disease stability on his most recent imaging, with a modest improvement in CEA/CA 19-9 and no evidence of new metastatic sites. His overall performance status remains ECOG 1, and he continues to manage his disease with good insight and strong family support.

1. METASTATIC COLORECTAL CANCER — DISEASE STABLE ON SECOND-LINE FOLFIRI/RAMUCIRUMAB
   - Imaging from the most recent restaging scan demonstrates no new lesions and marginal stability in known sites of disease. While formal RECIST criteria would classify this as stable disease, clinically we are pleased with the disease control achieved given the progressive nature of his illness and the challenges of his prior treatment course.
   - CEA has decreased from 142 ng/mL (at time of progression on maintenance) to 112 ng/mL — a reassuring downward trend and consistent with biological stability.
   - The RAISE trial established a median PFS of approximately 5.7 months and OS of approximately 13.3 months with FOLFIRI/ramucirumab in second-line mCRC; we are approaching that landmark and will need to continue close monitoring for evidence of further progression.
   - Plan: Continue FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab at current dose levels (irinotecan 150 mg/m2, ramucirumab 8 mg/kg, standard FOLFIRI backbone). Repeat restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis in approximately 8 weeks.
   - Tumor markers (CEA, CA 19-9) to be checked with each cycle.
   - Discussed the current trajectory with the patient in clear, accessible terms. He understands the disease is controlled but not cured. He is aware that if the disease grows further, there are additional lines of therapy we can consider (e.g., regorafenib, TAS-102 with or without bevacizumab), and he expressed willingness to continue treatment.

2. IRINOTECAN-RELATED DIARRHEA — MANAGEABLE AT REDUCED DOSE
   - Grade 2 delayed diarrhea was managed with loperamide and irinotecan dose reduction to 150 mg/m2; no further grade 2+ episodes since dose reduction implemented.
   - Patient reviewed on loperamide protocol and confirmed adherence; stools now 2-3 per day, manageable.
   - No dose escalation planned at this time.

3. RAMUCIRUMAB-RELATED HYPERTENSION — IMPROVED, CURRENTLY CONTROLLED
   - Blood pressure today 138/86 mmHg on triple antihypertensive regimen (amlodipine 10 mg, lisinopril 20 mg, hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg). This is acceptable control.
   - Ramucirumab was resumed at full dose (8 mg/kg) following the cycle 5 hold; no recurrence of grade 3 hypertension since.
   - Continue current antihypertensive regimen without change. Monitor BP at each infusion visit.
   - Cardiology co-management ongoing. Renal function surveillance via creatinine/urinalysis at regular intervals given lisinopril and antiangiogenic agents.

4. RESIDUAL PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY — IMPROVING
   - Oxaliplatin-induced sensorimotor neuropathy was grade 3 at its worst (cycle 10 of FOLFOX). Now significantly improved to grade 1 — patient reports intermittent tingling in bilateral feet only, no impact on ambulation or fine motor tasks.
   - Gabapentin being tapered (currently 300 mg BID from prior 300 mg TID). Plan to reduce further to 300 mg QHS over the next 4 weeks if symptoms continue to improve, with goal of discontinuation if grade resolves to 0.
   - Patient expressed satisfaction with the degree of recovery. EMG/NCS confirmed axonal pattern; full resolution remains uncertain but functional recovery is gratifying.

5. ANEMIA OF CHRONIC DISEASE
   - Hemoglobin stable at 10.9 g/dL. Not transfusion-dependent. No iron deficiency identified on prior workup.
   - No erythropoiesis-stimulating agent indicated given active antiangiogenic therapy. Monitor CBC with each treatment cycle.
   - If hemoglobin drops below 8.0 g/dL or patient becomes symptomatic, will reassess transfusion threshold.

6. TYPE 2 DIABETES MELLITUS
   - Well-controlled. HbA1c 6.9%, fasting glucose at home 110-140 mg/dL. Continue metformin 1000 mg BID.
   - Note: renal function stable, no need to hold metformin at this time.

7. GOALS OF CARE AND PALLIATIVE CARE INVOLVEMENT
   - Extensive discussion today with Mr. Delgado and his wife regarding disease trajectory, prognosis, and personal values. Patient was forthcoming and reflective — he understands the incurable nature of his disease and is primarily focused on maintaining function, spending time at home with his family, and continuing to teach part-time as long as feasible.
   - He expressed clear preferences: to avoid unnecessary hospitalizations, to continue active treatment as long as it is reasonably tolerated and providing benefit, and to plan thoughtfully for the future.
   - Outpatient palliative care consultation placed today. The palliative care team will co-manage symptom burden, quality-of-life issues, and advance care planning going forward.
   - Encouraged the patient and his wife to consider completing advance care planning documents (health care proxy designation, living will, POLST/MOLST) in the near future. Social work referral also placed to assist with logistical and psychosocial needs.
   - Hospice was not discussed as an imminent consideration given current ECOG PS 1, continued tolerance of treatment, and disease stability — but the concept was framed as an option for the future if and when treatment benefit diminishes or performance status declines significantly.

8. FOLLOW-UP:
   - Next infusion: in 2 weeks (FOLFIRI/ramucirumab cycle 9)
   - Next office visit: in 4 weeks for toxicity and symptom check
   - Next restaging CT CAP: in approximately 8 weeks
   - Palliative care: first appointment scheduled within 2 weeks
   - Labs (CBC, CMP, CEA, CA 19-9) with each cycle
   - Blood pressure monitoring at each infusion visit
   - Continue gabapentin taper as outlined

Time spent in face-to-face discussion, exam, and counseling: approximately 50 minutes. Greater than 50% of encounter time spent in counseling and coordination of care regarding disease trajectory, goals of care, toxicity management, and advance care planning.

Patient verbalized understanding of the plan. Questions answered to patient and family's satisfaction. All concerns addressed. Patient will contact the clinic via oncology nurse line for any urgent symptoms between visits.

— Medical Oncology, Attending Physician
   Electronically signed
